scenario: Helios Insurer authors FEEL guards on sequence flows across 6000 requests per day in Camunda 8.8. A developer migrating Java comparison logic into FEEL needs to know which equality operator the language uses so that comparisons on its exclusive gateways evaluate as intended at runtime in production.
question: Which operator does FEEL use for the equality comparison between two values in Camunda 8?

options.text: The double equals operator == is the equality operator in FEEL and Camunda 8 evaluates x == y to compare two values for equality at runtime
The single equals operator = is the equality operator in FEEL and Camunda 8 evaluates x = y to compare two values for equality at runtime
The keyword eq is the equality operator in FEEL and Camunda 8 evaluates x eq y to compare two values for equality at runtime
The triple equals operator === is the equality operator in FEEL and Camunda 8 evaluates x === y to compare two values for equality at runtime

a.text: Incorrect. The boolean expressions docs explicitly call out a caution that the FEEL equals operator has only one equals sign, unlike many other languages such as Java or JavaScript that use two.

b.text: Correct. The FEEL boolean expressions reference lists = as the equal-to operator for any type and includes an explicit caution that the operator has only one equals sign in FEEL.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not use an eq keyword for equality; the boolean expressions table lists = as the equal-to operator across all supported value types in the language.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no triple equals operator; equality is written with a single equals sign according to the boolean expressions reference, and there is no === variant in the language.

explanation: In FEEL the equality comparison uses a single equals sign (x = y), which differs from many other languages that use ==. The boolean expressions docs include an explicit caution that the FEEL equals operator has only one equals sign and supports comparing values of any matching type.
